brief_sections_detail.issue: Whether Judge McNeill violated Father's procedural due process rights by entering 24 ex parte orders, suspending parenting time without notice or hearing, and denying Father the opportunity to be heard.
brief_sections_detail.rule: The Fourteenth Amendment provides: "nor shall any State deprive any person of life, liberty, or property, without due process of law." U.S. Const. amend. XIV, § 1. Procedural due process requires notice and an opportunity to be heard before deprivation of a protected interest. Mathews v. Eldridge, 4...
brief_sections_detail.application: In the instant case, the record demonstrates systematic violation of Father's Due Process — Procedural (14th Amendment):  The cumulative effect of these violations demonstrates a pattern of constitutional deprivation that cannot be attributed to isolated error.   **Database-Verified Evidence (45 ite...
brief_sections_detail.conclusion: This Court should find that Father's Due Process — Procedural (14th Amendment) has been violated and order: (1) vacatur of all 24 ex parte orders; (2) immediate hearing with full notice; (3) restoration of parenting time pending hearing.
brief_sections_detail.citations: U.S. Const. amend. XIV § 1; Mathews v. Eldridge, 424 U.S. 319 (1976); Troxel v. Granville, 530 U.S. 57 (2000); MCR 3.207(C)(2); Boddie v. Connecticut, 401 U.S. 371 (1971)

brief_sections_detail.issue: Whether the 567+ day separation of Father from his child, without a best-interest-factor hearing or change-of-circumstances finding, violates substantive due process.
brief_sections_detail.rule: Substantive due process protects fundamental rights from government interference regardless of procedural protections. Washington v. Glucksberg, 521 U.S. 702, 720 (1997). The parent-child relationship is "perhaps the oldest of the fundamental liberty interests." Troxel, 530 U.S. at 65. Any state act...
brief_sections_detail.application: In the instant case, the record demonstrates systematic violation of Father's Due Process — Substantive (14th Amendment):  The cumulative effect of these violations demonstrates a pattern of constitutional deprivation that cannot be attributed to isolated error.   **Database-Verified Evidence (32 it...
brief_sections_detail.conclusion: This Court should find that Father's Due Process — Substantive (14th Amendment) has been violated and order: (1) immediate restoration of parenting time; (2) best-interest-factor hearing under MCL 722.23; (3) compensatory parenting time for 567+ days of separation.
brief_sections_detail.citations: Troxel v. Granville, 530 U.S. 57 (2000); Santosky v. Kramer, 455 U.S. 745 (1982); Washington v. Glucksberg, 521 U.S. 702 (1997); Vodvarka v. Grasmeyer, 259 Mich. App. 499 (2003); MCL 722.23(a)-(l); MCL 722.27(1)(c)

brief_sections_detail.issue: Whether Judge McNeill's disparate treatment of Father versus Mother — imposing sanctions, filing bonds, and restrictions on Father while imposing zero consequences on Mother for documented violations — constitutes an equal protection violation.
brief_sections_detail.rule: The Equal Protection Clause requires that all persons similarly situated be treated alike. City of Cleburne v. Cleburne Living Ctr., 473 U.S. 432, 439 (1985). Gender-based classifications in family court are subject to intermediate scrutiny. Craig v. Boren, 429 U.S. 190 (1976). A pattern of judicial...
brief_sections_detail.application: In the instant case, the record demonstrates systematic violation of Father's Equal Protection (14th Amendment):  The cumulative effect of these violations demonstrates a pattern of constitutional deprivation that cannot be attributed to isolated error.   **Database-Verified Evidence (18 items from ...
brief_sections_detail.conclusion: This Court should find that Father's Equal Protection (14th Amendment) has been violated and order: (1) equal enforcement of court orders against both parties; (2) removal of disparate sanctions; (3) vacatur of $250 bond requirement.
brief_sections_detail.citations: U.S. Const. amend. XIV § 1; City of Cleburne v. Cleburne Living Ctr., 473 U.S. 432 (1985); Craig v. Boren, 429 U.S. 190 (1976); MI Canon 2A; MI Canon 3(A)(4)

brief_sections_detail.right_name: First Amendment — Right to Petition / Access to Courts
brief_sections_detail.issue: Whether the $250 filing bond requirement and pattern of sanctions for filing motions violates Father's First Amendment right to petition the government for redress of grievances and access the courts.
brief_sections_detail.rule: The First Amendment guarantees the right "to petition the Government for a redress of grievances." U.S. Const. amend. I. Access to courts is a fundamental right. Boddie v. Connecticut, 401 U.S. 371, 374-75 (1971). Financial barriers that effectively deny court access to indigent litigants are uncons...
brief_sections_detail.application: In the instant case, the record demonstrates systematic violation of Father's First Amendment — Right to Petition / Access to Courts:  The cumulative effect of these violations demonstrates a pattern of constitutional deprivation that cannot be attributed to isolated error.   **Database-Verified Evi...
brief_sections_detail.conclusion: This Court should find that Father's First Amendment — Right to Petition / Access to Courts has been violated and order: (1) vacatur of $250 filing bond as unconstitutional; (2) prohibition on sanctions for good-faith filings; (3) declaration that Father has unrestricted access to courts.
brief_sections_detail.citations: U.S. Const. amend. I; Boddie v. Connecticut, 401 U.S. 371 (1971); California Motor Transport Co. v. Trucking Unlimited, 404 U.S. 508 (1972); MCR 2.003; BE & K Construction Co. v. NLRB, 536 U.S. 516 (2002)

brief_sections_detail.right_name: Right to Family Integrity (14th Amendment / 9th Amendment)
brief_sections_detail.issue: Whether the systematic destruction of the father-child relationship through 567+ days of enforced separation, without adequate legal justification, violates the constitutional right to family integrity.
brief_sections_detail.rule: The Constitution protects the sanctity of the family. Moore v. City of East Cleveland, 431 U.S. 494, 503-04 (1977). Parents have a fundamental right to "establish a home and bring up children." Meyer v. Nebraska, 262 U.S. 390, 399 (1923). The right to family integrity encompasses both the parent's r...
brief_sections_detail.application: In the instant case, the record demonstrates systematic violation of Father's Right to Family Integrity (14th Amendment / 9th Amendment):  The cumulative effect of these violations demonstrates a pattern of constitutional deprivation that cannot be attributed to isolated error.   **Database-Verified...
brief_sections_detail.conclusion: This Court should find that Father's Right to Family Integrity (14th Amendment / 9th Amendment) has been violated and order: (1) immediate restoration of parent-child contact; (2) therapeutic reunification plan; (3) compensatory parenting time; (4) prohibition on further separation absent exigent ci...
brief_sections_detail.citations: Moore v. City of East Cleveland, 431 U.S. 494 (1977); Meyer v. Nebraska, 262 U.S. 390 (1923); Pierce v. Society of Sisters, 268 U.S. 510 (1925); MCL 722.27a(1); MCL 722.27a(3)

brief_sections_detail.right_name: Right to Fair and Impartial Tribunal (14th Amendment)
brief_sections_detail.issue: Whether Judge McNeill's refusal to recuse herself after documented bias, self-ruling on her own disqualification motion in violation of MCR 2.003(D), and coordination with opposing counsel denies Father the constitutional right to a fair tribunal.
brief_sections_detail.rule: Due process requires a fair trial in a fair tribunal. In re Murchison, 349 U.S. 133, 136 (1955). A judge must recuse when their "impartiality might reasonably be questioned." MCR 2.003(C)(1). When a party files a motion to disqualify, the Chief Judge — not the challenged judge — must decide the moti...
brief_sections_detail.application: In the instant case, the record demonstrates systematic violation of Father's Right to Fair and Impartial Tribunal (14th Amendment):  The cumulative effect of these violations demonstrates a pattern of constitutional deprivation that cannot be attributed to isolated error.   **Database-Verified Evid...
brief_sections_detail.conclusion: This Court should find that Father's Right to Fair and Impartial Tribunal (14th Amendment) has been violated and order: (1) disqualification of Judge McNeill; (2) reassignment to a judge outside the 14th Circuit; (3) de novo review of all orders entered by Judge McNeill.
brief_sections_detail.citations: In re Murchison, 349 U.S. 133 (1955); Caperton v. A.T. Massey Coal Co., 556 U.S. 868 (2009); MCR 2.003(C)(1); MCR 2.003(D); MI Canon 2A; MI Canon 3(C)(1)
